coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising nanomaterials due to their tunable fluorescence, high biocompatibility, and diverse applications in bioimaging, diagnostics, and theranostics. Sustainable synthesis approaches utilizing plant biomass-derived carbon sources, such as cellulose, citric acid, and terephthalic acid derivatives, offer an eco-friendly alternative to conventional methods. Here, we report a facile one-pot solvothermal synthesis of novel nitrogen-doped CQDs (ATA100) using 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) as an alternative to biomass-based carbon and nitrogen precursor, with 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) as a surface passivation agent. Nitrogen doping enhances fluorescence quantum yield to 38 %, outperforming citric acid-derived CQDs (CA100). Structural characterization via Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), proton nuclear magnetic resonance (¹H NMR), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS) confirms the formation of uniform nanoparticles (<5 nm) with enriched amine functionalities. Biocompatibility assessments reveal low cytotoxicity and enhanced cellular uptake, with 2.6-fold higher fluorescence in A549 cancer cells compared to normal chondrocytes, demonstrating its potential as an efficient bioimaging agent. Moreover, ATA100 exhibits fluorescence quenching upon catalase interaction, while its fluorescence intensity increases in response to hydrogen peroxide, highlighting its application for reactive oxygen species (ROS) detection in oxidative stress-related diseases. This study underscores the industrial feasibility of nitrogen-doped CQDs for large-scale bioimaging, biosensing, and therapeutic monitoring. By leveraging renewable resources, ATA100 presents a sustainable, eco-friendly and scalable alternative to traditional semiconductor-based fluorescent probes, advancing bio-inspired nanomaterials for precision medicine and industrial diagnostics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) High-resolution TEM image of synthesized ATA100 CQDs, highlighting their morphology. The inset shows a magnified view of a single ATA100 CQD. (b) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis indicates the average hydrodynamic diameter of ATA100 CQDs as 2.26 nm. (c) High-resolution TEM image of synthesized CA100 CQDs, highlighting their morphology. (d) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis indicates the average hydrodynamic diameter of CA100 CQDs as 3.6 nm.
(a) FTIR spectra comparing functional groups of ATA100 (black) and CA100 (red). (b) ¹H NMR spectra of ATA100. (c) ¹H NMR spectra of CA100, confirming structural composition.

(a) Absorption spectra of ATA100 (black) and CA100 (red). (b) 2D fluorescence excitation-emission spectra of ATA100 and CA100. (c) Excitation spectra, dots line, ATA100 (black) and CA100 (red), with corresponding emission spectra in solid line (d) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves for ATA100 (black) and CA100 (red), demonstrating their photophysical properties.
Photostability test under 365 nm UV light (a) and daylight (b). The inset shows the CQDs images under UV light and daylight.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of catalase upon interaction with increasing concentrations of ATA100 (0–6.5 μg/mL in 0.5 μg/mL intervals). (b-d) Excitation and emission spectra of (b) ATA100 alone, (c) catalase alone, and (d) catalase with ATA100. (e) Circular dichroism (CD) spectra showing structural changes in catalase upon binding to ATA100.

(a-b) Cytotoxicity assay of ATA100 and CA100 CQDs on A549 cells. (c-d) Cytotoxicity assay of ATA100 and CA100 CQDs on human chondrocyte cells. Cell viability is normalized to the control (100 % viable). Assay used: Cell Culture Reagent SF.

Confocal fluoresscence images showing cellular uptake of ATA100 and CA100 CQDs. The first column represents the nucleus (red), followed by CQDs fluorescence (blue). The merged images illustrate intracellular localization of CQDs within the cells.

(a) Confocal microscopy images showing fluorescence distribution of ATA100 and CA100 in A549 and chondrocyte cells. (b) Quantitative comparison of fluorescence intensities in A549 and chondrocyte cells. ATA concentration: 600 µg/mL.Scale:10 µm.

(a) Fluorescence quenching effect of ATA100 in A549 cells upon exposure to increasing concentrations of hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) after 30 minutes of incubation. (b) Quantitative changes in fluorescence intensity of ATA100 in A549 cells as a function of hydrogen peroxide concentration.Scale:10 µm.
Schematic representation of the synthesis process of ATA100 and CA100 CQDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising nanomaterials due to their tunable fluorescence, high biocompatibility, and diverse applications in bioimaging, diagnostics, and theranostics. Sustainable synthesis approaches utilizing plant biomass-derived carbon sources, such as cellulose, citric acid, and terephthalic acid derivatives, offer an eco-friendly alternative to conventional methods. Here, we report a facile one-pot solvothermal synthesis of novel nitrogen-doped CQDs (ATA100) using 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) as an alternative to biomass-based carbon and nitrogen precursor, with 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) as a surface passivation agent. Nitrogen doping enhances fluorescence quantum yield to 38 %, outperforming citric acid-derived CQDs (CA100). Structural characterization via Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), proton nuclear magnetic resonance (¹H NMR), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS) confirms the formation of uniform nanoparticles (<5 nm) with enriched amine functionalities. Biocompatibility assessments reveal low cytotoxicity and enhanced cellular uptake, with 2.6-fold higher fluorescence in A549 cancer cells compared to normal chondrocytes, demonstrating its potential as an efficient bioimaging agent. Moreover, ATA100 exhibits fluorescence quenching upon catalase interaction, while its fluorescence intensity increases in response to hydrogen peroxide, highlighting its application for reactive oxygen species (ROS) detection in oxidative stress-related diseases. This study underscores the industrial feasibility of nitrogen-doped CQDs for large-scale bioimaging, biosensing, and therapeutic monitoring. By leveraging renewable resources, ATA100 presents a sustainable, eco-friendly and scalable alternative to traditional semiconductor-based fluorescent probes, advancing bio-inspired nanomaterials for precision medicine and industrial diagnostics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a class of zero-dimensional carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials that have gained significant attention due to their outstanding optical and physicochemical properties. These include low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, high chemical stability, superior water solubility, and environment friendly synthesis methods, making them highly suitable for diverse applications in sensing, ion detection, electrocatalysis, photobleaching resistance, fluorescence materials, and bioimaging (Liu et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2004; Medintz et al., 2005). Over the years, researchers have developed various CQDs, optimizing their fluorescence emission characteristics, which can be easily tuned by varying synthetic parameters and precursor materials (Singaravelu et al., 2021; Zong et al., 2024; Ozyurt et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2004). One of the key aspects of CQD synthesis is the selection of an appropriate carbon source, as it influences the material's availability, stability, and biocompatibility. In recent years, biomass-derived precursors have gained prominence due to their renewable nature and alignment with sustainable production practices (Malode et al., 2023). CQDs have been successfully synthesized from various natural sources, including eggs (Zhang et al., 2015), milk (Wang and Zhou, 2014), orange juice (Sahu et al., 2012), and agricultural and forestry residues (Liang et al., 2014). Among these, purified biomass-derived carbon sources, such as cellulose, citric acid, terephthalic acid, and their derivatives, have attracted considerable attention due to their high molecular activity, enhanced biocompatibility, and potential for large-scale commercialization. However, these carbon sources continue to face challenges including availability, multiple purification steps and mostly have poor fluorescence properties. Doping CQDs with heteroatoms, particularly nitrogen, has been extensively explored to modify their electronic and optical properties, thereby expanding their application potential (Lu et al., 2015; Zhai et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2014). Studies have shown that nitrogen doping significantly enhances fluorescence intensity, making these materials highly suitable for biological applications. For instance, Zhang et al., (2012) synthesized N-doped CQDs with tunable luminescence by controlling nitrogen content, while Qian et al. (2014) reported a fluorescence enhancement due to the formation of polyaromatic structures induced by nitrogen doping. Similarly, Sun et al. (2014) developed highly luminescent N-doped CQDs with improved quantum yield, attributing the fluorescence enhancement to increased electron density distribution at Fermi levels, leading to hypsochromic or bathochromic shifts in absorption and emission spectra (Kumar and Agnihotri, 2024a; Santiago et al., 2014).

Common nitrogen doping sources include aromatic amines (Lu et al., 2015) and aliphatic amines such as ethylenediamine, poly(ethylenediamine), and trimethylamine (Zhai et al., 2012), which improve biological affinity, imaging resolution, and photostability (Kumar et al., 2024b; Abu et al., 2023). However, the development of alternative bio-based nitrogen sources and optimized synthesis methods remains an area of great interest, as it can further enhance the properties of CQDs for safe and effective use in bioimaging, drug delivery, and theranostic applications. Despite these advances, systematic studies focusing on tuning fluorescence emission from readily available biomass-based materials remain limited and highly desirable. In this study, we report a facile one-pot solvothermal synthesis of novel nitrogen-doped CQDs (ATA100) using 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) as an alternate source of bio-mass carbon and nitrogen precursor and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) surface passivation agent, respectively ( Scheme 1). We chose this as combination because they both are stable, commercially available, an alternative to bio-mass carbon source and a self-nitrogen doping agent, respectively. In addition, the amine group of EDA reacts with the carboxylic group of ATA and facilitates the incorporation of nitrogen into the carbon lattice of CQDs, offering a sustainable approach to enhance CQD fluorescence properties (Bandi et al., 2020; Qu et al., 2014) as we have illustrated in Fig S1. We systematically investigate its impact on fluorescence properties, cellular uptake, biosensing potential, cytotoxicity, nano-bio interactions, and protein therapeutics (Debnath et al., 2024; Zheng et al., 2023; Krasley et al., 2024; Chinen et al., 2014).

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) play a dual role in cancer, supporting cell signaling and homeostasis under normal conditions but driving genomic instability, tumor progression, drug resistance, and metastasis when dysregulated. Cancer cells exhibit elevated ROS due to mitochondrial dysfunction and oncogenic signaling, making ROS modulation a key factor in therapy response. Catalase (CAT), an antioxidant enzyme, regulates ROS by decomposing hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) into water and oxygen (Gebicka and Madej, 2019). Its expression varies in tumors, some cancers upregulate catalase to counter oxidative stress, while others have reduced levels, increasing their susceptibility to ROS-induced apoptosis. Dysregulated catalase has been linked to lung, breast, and colorectal cancers (Nandi et al., 2019), and acatalasemia further heightens oxidative stress, promoting tumorigenesis. Given ROS’s role in cancer, precise detection is essential for understanding tumor microenvironments and advancing targeted therapies. Here, we introduce nitrogen-doped ATA100 CQDs as fluorescent ROS sensors, exhibiting quenching upon catalase interaction and enhanced fluorescence in hydrogen peroxide. By studying these interactions in A549 lung cancer cells, we highlight ATA100’s potential as a sensitive and selective ROS marker for real-time oxidative stress monitoring in cancer diagnostics and treatment. Taken together, we present the one-pot solvothermal synthesis of ATA100 CQDs, their characterization as ultrasmall nanoparticles with remarkable fluorescence quantum yield and biocompatability demonstrated using A549 lung cancer cells and normal chondrocytes. Our biocompatible and non-toxic fluorescent material holds potential applications in cell tracking, drug delivery monitoring, tissue imaging, and biomarker-based disease diagnostics. The scalable and sustainable nature of ATA100 synthesis further enhances its industrial feasibility for bioimaging, biosensing, and nanomedicine applications.

The materials used in the experiment were ultra-pure water (Milli-Q; resistivity of 18.2 mΩ cm−1) sourced from Fisher Scientific in the United Kingdom, 2-amineterephthalic acid (ATA) ethylenediamine (EDA) obtained from TCI, Japan, Dimethylformamide (DMF) and absolute ethanol (EtOH) purchased from Merck in Germany, citric acid obtained from Merck in Germany. We purchased phosphate buffer from Sigma-Aldrich, a company based in Germany. The Hoechst 33342 Solution (62249) and BioTracker 650 Red Nuclear Dye (SCT119) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich, a reputable chemical supplier in the United States. Hydrothermal reactor (50 mL) from CGOLDENWALL.

ATA100 was synthesized from 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) in DMF/deionized water (3:1) by the hydrothermal method. Optimization of reaction conditions are listed in supporting information (table S1). In a 100 mL beaker, ATA (0.47 g) was dissolved in 15 mL DMF + 5 mL deionized water and then sonicated for 5 min until a clear pale yellow-colored solution was obtained then added ethylene diamine (EDA, 0.17 mL). This solution was placed in a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor (50 mL capacity) and heated at 230 °C, 10 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered using a PTFLE microporous membrane (size = 0.22 mm) to obtain a yellow solution. Then, DMF was completely evaporated. After that, the extraction method was performed 3 times through a chloroform solvent to remove unreacted ATA molecules. The aqueous layer was centrifuged (12000 rpm, 30 min) to remove high-weight carbon aggregates. The filtration process was repeated twice to eliminate particles that were larger in size. The CQDs were purified using a 1 kDa dialysis tube for 48 hours and then evaporated at −20°C by vacuum lyophilization to obtain the solid form of CQDs. For further usage, the CQDs were diluted in deionized water as desired. Similar hydrothermal and purification method was used to prepare CA100 from citric acid (CA) and EDA.

The JASCO FP-8300 Fluorescence Spectrometer was used to measure the CQDs' emission spectra. On a JASCO FP-8300 spectrofluorometer set at room temperature, emission spectra between 300 and 600 nm were observed. With different excitation wavelengths, the emission spectra were captured at a scan rate of 1000 nm per minute. The obtained dried powder of CQDs was dissolved into ultrapure water to make CQDs working solution (1 mg/mL). 100 µL of CQDs working solution was diluted with 5 mL of ultrapure water and vortexed. Then, their photophysical properties were studied. A Hamamatsu Photonics Quantaurus-QY C11347–11Absolute PL Quantum Yield Spectrometer was used to measure the quantum yields, and the integrated measurement software was used to calculate them. The UV–visible spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35, USA) simultaneously recorded their absorbance in the 200 nm to 500 nm range. Photostability test under UV conditions: UV light source: SLUV-4 (AS ONE), UV light intensity: 743 µW/cm2, Cell size: 10 x 10 x 1 mm3, UV Source and sample distance = 5 cm and UV wavelength 365 nm. White light exposure was carried under normal day light for 9 days. The functional groups on the CQDs were identified using a Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) and Bruker NMR AVANCE-III, 500 mhz. The CQD`s particle size was determined by using high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) from Philips in the USA and dynamic light scattering method in water (Malvern Zetasizer, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Culture proliferation of human chondrocyte and A549 cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: The primary mammalian cell line, the Normal Human Knee Articular Chondrocytes (NHAC-kn) cell line, was purchased from Lonza (Catalog no: CC-2550). Chondrocytes were grown and cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with 4500 mg/L D-Glucose without L-glutamine along with 10 % Fetal bovine serum (FBS) (Sigma Aldrich product number: F7524 of 500 mL),1 % Penicillin/streptomycin (50,000 units/mL), HEPES (1 M) and sodium pyruvate solution. The adhered cell culture was maintained in the 25 cm2 and 75 cm2 cell culture flask (ThermoFisher Scientific), along with a seeding density of 2.5 × 105- 3.5 × 105 for regular passage. For the chondrocyte expansion, the two essential growth factors, basic fibroblast growth factor (FGF- 5 ng/mL) and Transforming Growth Factor ß (TGF ß −1 1 ng/mL), were added to the fresh media in the culture flask, and standard conditions of 37ºC and 5 % CO2 were kept. Cells were observed, and a media change was performed every second day. Also, adenocarcinoma human alveolar basal epithelial cells (A549), a lung cancerous cell line, were maintained and culture in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) along with 10 % Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % P/S according to the standard protocol. (Cooper et al.,2016; Gomez-Camarillo et al., 2009).

To perform the toxicity assay of the compounds, the calorimetric assay kit was used (Cell count Reagent SF assay kit; Product No 07553–15). This assay was performed per the manufacturer’s instructions: Nacalai Tesque, Inc., “Cell Count Reagent SF protocol.” Cells were trypsinized with 1X Trypsin/EDTA and 5000 chondrocyte cells/well (100 µL of suspension), were seeded on a 96-well plate, and kept for 24 hours at a 37ºC incubator. A similar seeding density was maintained for A549 cells. The following day, cells were treated with increasing concentrations of the test substance (toxicant) and sustained for the desired period (24 hours, 48 hours, and such). Thereafter, the cells were treated with the cell culture SF reagent for 2–4 hours, and the absorbance at 450 nm was measured on the microplate reader (Tecan: Infinite® F50 Plus) with 600 nm as the reference wavelength. The formula used to calculate cell viability is as follows:
Cell Viability (%) = (Absorbance of Sample /Absorbance of control) × 100.
The average ± standard deviation was plotted to validate the results of cell viability experiments performed in three biological replicates.

To validate the carbon dots for their ability to fluorescence and uptake into the cellular environment, confocal fluorescence scanning microscopy was performed. For the confocal imaging, the cells were passaged with T/E (0.05 %), and the pellet was resuspended in the fresh complete media. The cell counting was performed manually using a glass slide hemocytometer with Neubauer chamber and cell number such that 15–18 x103 chondrocyte cells whereas 10–12 x103 A549 cells were seeded in a 35 mm glass bottom dish (IWAKI- 3961–035) making a final volume of 200 µL. On day two, the cells were given treatment with optimum concentrations of our material (ATA100). After incubation for 24 hours, cells were washed 3–4 times with PBS and stained with nucleus stain, BioTracker 650 Red Nuclear Dye (Product number: SCT119), observing at 10X and higher magnifications. (Jhonsi, 2018)

To contrast and compare the two carbon dots, the fluorescent intensity was calculated by using the software Image J. For the cellular bioimages, the various selected positions of the same area, height, and width were measured, and the average of mean integrated intensity was plotted as the fluorescence intensity. Image J analysis was performed in the three replicates. Thereafter, in order to observe the change in the fluorescence of the material after being uptaken by the cell environment, the material fluorescence was observed on a glass side with a specified cover slip under a microscope, and the cell images were chosen for a fixed dimension area where the mean average of intensities was plotted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen peroxide detection assay and bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: For the hydrogen peroxide detection assay, A549 cells were seeded for 24 hours in a 35 mm glass bottom dish. Post 24 hours, the dishes were treated with various concentrations of hydrogen peroxide (20 µM, 50 µM, 70 µM, 100 µM) and incubated at 37°C for 30 minutes. Following that, the cell medium was changed with the ATA100 and again incubated for 30 mins. Post one-hour treatment of hydrogen peroxide and ATA100; confocal imaging was performed. (Phukan et al.,2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be synthesized using various methods, including laser ablation, arc discharge, electrochemical oxidation, hydrothermal treatment, and microwave pyrolysis. Hydrothermal treatment is widely preferred due to its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, non-toxic nature, and sustainability, making it a viable option for large-scale production and industrial applications. In this study, we successfully synthesized ATA100 CQDs from 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) and CA100 CQDs from citric acid (CA) using a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. This approach involved dissolving the precursors in a hydrothermal reactor with 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) as a passivation agent and heating the solution under controlled conditions. The well-established synthesis of CA100 utilizes citric acid as a carbon source, where its carboxyl functional groups facilitate dehydration and carbonization, while EDA enhances surface passivation and stability. ATA100 was synthesized under identical reaction conditions to CA100 to systematically analyze nitrogen doping's effects on the optical and physicochemical properties of CQDs. ATA100 benefits from self-nitrogen doping, as ATA serves as both a carbon and nitrogen source, leading to improved fluorescence properties and enhanced electronic characteristics. Comprehensive characterization of the synthesized CQDs was conducted using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), proton nuclear magnetic resonance (¹H NMR), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), UV–visible spectroscopy, and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM and DLS characterization of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) method were employed to assess the morphological structure of the ATA100 and CA100. The solution in water was dropped onto the carbon grid and left to dry at room temperature. The HR-TEM images ( Fig. 1a) show that ATA100 forms well-defined spherical nanostructures with no significant aggregation, confirming uniform dispersion. DLS measurements (Fig. 1b) indicate that ATA100 has an average hydrodynamic diameter of less than 5 nm, highlighting its excellent colloidal stability, which is essential for biomedical applications. Similarly, Fig. 1(c) represents HR-TEM image of CA100. DLS analysis of CA100 indicates the average hydrodynamic diameter is larger than ATA100. (Fig. 1d)

The functional groups on the CQD surface were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy ( Fig. 2a). The vibration band at 3380 cm−1 is attributed to N–H stretching of amine. The broad vibrational band at 3700–2500 cm−1 indicates the presence of O–H stretching of carboxylic acid. (Lu et al., 2017) A shoulder peak of N–H bending of amine at 1630 cm−1 and CO of carboxylic acid stretching at 1570 cm−1 appeared. (Yuan et al., 2018) The differences in the intensity and appearance of peaks were observed in these two spectra ATA100 and CA100, as shown in Fig. 2(a). It suggests that the content of functional group such as CO, OH and NH group on their surface are different. The FTIR spectra of ATA100 show a strong peak at N-H stretching and NH bending region as compared to CA100 suggesting the presence of more amine groups on their surface. In addition, two peaks at 1360 and 1120 cm−1 peaks contribute to C–N and C–O group formation in the CQDs (Vervald et al., 2025). Furthermore, the intensity peaks of C–H stretching of the aromatic ring in the range of 3067–3019 cm−1 of CA100 have low intensity compared to ATA100. The changes reveal that there is more in π-π* stacking interaction of the aromatic ring on the creation of carbon cores in ATA100. Overall, the evaluation of the above FTIR spectra illustrated that ATA100 was successfully formed during the hydrothermal process, having more amine functional groups, including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, on the surface of the CQD (Ansi and Renuka, 2019; Wei et al., 2020).

Proton Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H NMR) characterization of ATA and CQDs were performed in a deuterium oxide (D2O) solvent are shown in Fig. 2(b) and Fig.S2. TEM, FT-TR and 1H NMR data corroborated with each other. 1H NMR spectroscopy analysis also clearly revealed the formation of carbon dot (Bartolomei et al., 2022; Morbiato et al., 2025). For example, 1H NMR showed the most significant signals in the range of 8–6 ppm, assigned for the hydrogen of aromatic rings and 4–1 ppm for aliphatic protons, which is precisely supported by C-H stretching and bending vibration spectra of FT-IR. While comparing 1H NMR spectra of ATA with ATA100, a signal of ATA aromatic hydrogen at , 7.89-7.44 ppm, disappeared and a new peak at 8–6 ppm at aromatic region of ATA100 appeared. It suggested that the benzene ring in ATA was carbonized and formed the new carbonized structure of CQDs under high temperature and pressure (Zivkovic et al., 2016). In addition, the new signal at aliphatic region 4.24–1.14 ppm was observed that corresponded to the various hydrogen of aliphatic carbons attached with amine, ether, hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and aliphatic groups, respectively, on the surface of CQDs (Hong et al., 2018).

Similarly, 1H NMR of CA100 (Fig. 2c) showed that the spectra of CA and EDA peaks of the hydrogen aliphatic region at 4–1 ppm disappeared and few new peaks at 8–6 ppm at aromatic region and more peaks at 4–1 ppm was observed. It is suggested that aliphatic peak of CA and EDA was carbonized and formed CQDs under high temperature and pressure (Mohammed et al., 2023). These observations validate the efficient surface functionalization of ATA100, which contributes to its improved dispersibility and optical properties.

In addition to the FTIR and NMR spectra, a UV–vis absorption study was performed to detect CQD formation, changes in the surface functionality and understand their optical properties as shown in Fig. 3. Steady state absorption spectroscopy was presented to investigate the correlation between the CQD composition and optical properties. The ATA100 CQD's absorption, excitation and fluorescence spectra in Fig. 3 reveal that they absorb light between 250 and 450 nm and emit it between 300 and 600 nm. The absorption spectra contain some major bands as shown in Fig. 3(a) including a strong absorption at wavelengths shorter than 300 nm attributed to the presence of CC bonds caused by the π-π* electron transition. Similarly, the peak observed at 340 nm was ascribed to the n-π* transition of the non-bonding electrons in C-C, C-N, and CO. 2D fluorescence spectra in Fig. 3(b) revealed excitation wavelength was correlated with the emission fluorescence band. For example, in ATA100 (Fig. 3c), the maximum intensity of the emission band at 415 nm was associated with 300 nm excitation wavelength. In the case of CA100, the maximum intensity of emission band was at 440 nm was related with 400 nm excitation wavelength. So, it can be conferred that both CQDs revealed that their fluorescence band lies in the blue emission region. In case of ATA100 absorption, excitation and emission spectra are blue shifted as compared to CA100. For example, in ATA100, absorption, excitation and emission maxima are 300, 310 and 415 nm respectively. Here, absorption, excitation and emission spectra are blue shifted by 40, 30 and 25 nm respectively compared to CA100. Interestingly, it was found that ATA100 had a stronger fluorescent enhancement (emission efficiency = 38 %) than CA100 (emission efficiency = 23 %). It demonstrates that doping extra nitrogen from ATA significantly enhances carbon dot's fluorescence. There is still no convincing evidence of the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of nitrogen-doped CQDs reported. One of the speculative explanations is that the incorporation of nitrogen might create defect structures in the carbonization process of CQD. It might break the large, conjugated carbon structure, which would lead to the formation of more polyaromatic structures and give rise to more substantial emissions. So, this explanation describes to some extent why there is an enhancement in the emission efficiency of N-doped ATA100 over CA100 CQDs. This observation correlates with the findings of the literature (Kumar and Agnihotri, 2024a; Ding et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014).

To investigate the fluorescence dynamics of ATA100 and CA100, the fluorescence decay profile was measured (Fig. 3d). Each fluorescence decay trace was analyzed individually as a sum of two exponential functions in terms of decay times τi and the associated pre-exponential factors αi. Mono-exponential decay functions are not suitable to describe the observed decays as evidenced by the large χ2 value and high non-random residuals. The fluorescence decay of ATA100 at 415 nm displays bi-exponential behavior and was used to fit emission wavelength. The fast component (τ1 = 1.94 ns) has an amplitude of about 1.7 %, whereas the contribution of τ2 (14.1 ns) component is 98.3 %. In the case of CA100, the fluorescence decay in water at 440 nm also displays bi-exponential behavior was used to fit emission wavelength. The fast component (τ1 = 2.5 ns) has an amplitude of about 7.95 %, whereas the contribution of τ2 (13.8 ns) component is 92.05 %. It can be seen that both case τ2 lifetimes have the largest contribution. Also, an average fluorescent lifetime of both CQDs is calculated. Introducing extra self-nitrogen-doped carbon dots on ATA100 reveals a nearly 1 ns slower lifetime with CA100. It inferred the critical role of high-content nitrogen in fluorescence performance. As a result, ATA100 shows improvement of quantum yield (Xu et al., 2013).

Taken together, our results demonstrate that ATA100 CQDs, synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal process, offer superior structural and optical properties compared to conventional CA100 CQDs. The nitrogen doping enhances fluorescence efficiency, making ATA100 a promising material for bioimaging, biosensing, and theranostic applications. Moreover, its sustainable synthesis using biomass-derived precursors reinforces its industrial relevance for scalable and environmentally friendly nanomaterial production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability test under UV and white light
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the photostability of CQDs, we conducted an irradiation experiment using both white and UV light. For the UV light exposure, we utilized 365 nm UV light over a specified duration (90 minutes) ( Fig. 4a). The results indicated that ATA100 remained highly stable under UV light, even after 90 minutes, while CA100 showed significant degradation (50 %) during the same period (Fig. 4b). For day light exposure both CQDs are stable fluorescence up to 9 days. It is speculated that the presence of nitrogen group can lead to formation of various groups such as amide and amine on the surface of CQDs. These group can act as stabilizers that can protect the carbon core from UV light. (Liu et al., 2016; Wang et al.,2015)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nano-bio interaction between ATA100 and catalase
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of CQDs with catalase is of growing interest due to its implications in ROS modulation, oxidative stress monitoring, and potential theranostic applications in cancer biology. Several studies have investigated CAT interactions with molecules such as surfactants, fluoroquinolones, chrysoidine, and carbon nanotubes (Qin and Liu, 2013; Yang et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2013; Baker et al., 2023; Chhe et al., 2022). However, there is limited knowledge regarding the binding effects of CQDs on the structural integrity and enzymatic activity of CAT. This section explores the in vitro interaction between ATA100 CQDs and catalase to better understand their biocompatibility, binding mechanisms, and enzymatic influence.

To assess catalase binding to ATA100, fluorescence quenching experiments were conducted with increasing ATA100 concentrations (0–6.5 μg mL⁻¹, with 0.5 μg mL⁻¹ increments), using an excitation wavelength of 280 nm ( Fig. 5a). The results indicate that catalase fluorescence intensity, with an emission peak at 335 nm, progressively decreases as ATA100 concentration increases. Additionally, a redshift (∼7 nm) in emission maximum suggests electronic perturbation of the catalase microenvironment due to CQD binding. Catalase comprises 545 amino acids and functions as a tetramer containing three intrinsic fluorophores-tryptophan, tyrosine, and phenylalanine-contributing to its fluorescence. The observed quenching suggests a strong interaction between ATA100 and catalase, likely mediated through non-covalent interactions such as hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. Additionally, fluorescence enhancement of ATA100 beyond 400 nm upon catalase binding suggests possible conformational changes influencing CQD electronic properties. The excitation-emission matrix spectra (Fig. 5b-d) provide further insights into the binding mechanism. Peaks A and B correspond to aromatic amino acids and peptide bonds of catalase, while peaks C and D originate from ATA100 fluorescence. The consistent spectral shifts confirm the occurrence of direct ATA100-catalase interactions without significant structural disruption. Circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy was performed to validate that ATA100 does not induce major conformational changes in catalase (Fig. 5e). The results indicate no substantial α-helical alterations occur upon ATA100 binding, supporting its high biocompatibility and minimal structural interference with the enzyme. This behavior contrasts with previous studies using heavy metal-based quantum dots (e.g., Cd and CdTe), which induced significant tryptophan quenching and conformational perturbations even at nanomolar concentrations (Sun et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2019). Our results suggest that ATA100 interacts with catalase in a biocompatible manner, maintaining enzyme structural integrity while modulating its fluorescence and highlight the potential of ATA100 as a ROS-responsive fluorescent probe for oxidative stress monitoring and biosensing applications,

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological evaluation of ATA100 verified enhanced fluorescent uptake and biocompatibility
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the biocompatibility and non-toxic nature of ATA100, the cell culture count reagent SF cytotoxicity assay was carried out. Ensuring the safe entry of materials into the intracellular environment is essential for various biological and biomedical applications, particularly in bioimaging, drug delivery, and sub-cellular tracking (Augustine et al., 2020; Wolfbeis et al., 2015). The cytotoxicity of ATA100 and CA100 was evaluated in A549 lung cancer cells and normal chondrocyte cells over 24 and 48 hours following a standard protocol. A concentration range of 200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000 µg/mL was tested, and cytotoxicity assays were conducted in triplicates. The results, plotted as mean ± standard deviation, indicated that both ATA100 and CA100 exhibited no significant cytotoxic effects, even at 1000 µg/mL after 48 hours. However, ATA100 demonstrated nearly 100 % viability in A549 cells, while chondrocyte cell viability remained 82 % at 600 µg/mL ( Fig. 6), reinforcing ATA100's high biocompatibility.

Having established ATA100’s compatibility with biological systems, its cellular uptake and imaging potential were examined (Augustine et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020; Ali et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2024b). Confocal fluorescence microscopy revealed successful internalization of ATA100 and CA100 into A549 cells ( Fig. 7). Chondrocyte cells incubated with CQDs exhibited bright fluorescence in the blue and red channels upon excitation at 405 nm and 543 nm, respectively. In contrast, control cells exhibited negligible fluorescence under the same conditions. CQDs were primarily localized in the cytoplasm near the nucleus, with no observed blinking or photobleaching. Confocal fluorescence bioimaging studies of A549 and chondrocyte cells treated with ATA100 and CA100 alone without the commercially available staining dyes further verified the uptake of CQDs in the cytoplasm of the cellular microenvironment (Fig. S3). While both CQDs exhibited photostability and were non-toxic, ATA100 treatment had a relatively higher fluorescence than that observed with CA100 treatment. Nuclear staining using BioTracker 650 Red further confirmed intracellular localization. Confocal images (Fig. 7) showed a clear contrast between the red-stained nuclei and the blue fluorescence from ATA100, demonstrating efficient cellular uptake at an optimal concentration of 600 µg/mL.

A key property of CQDs for biomedical applications is their fluorescence intensity after cellular uptake. Image J analysis revealed that ATA100 exhibited significantly higher fluorescence than CA100, attributable to its enhanced optical properties and nitrogen doping. Adenocarcinoma human alveolar basal epithelial cells (A549) produce elevated Reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels as they depend on it for growth, survival, and metastasis (Nandi et al., 2019). This results in a higher metabolic rate and altered antioxidant defense mechanisms, making them more vulnerable to oxidative stress and facilitating more precise detection of ROS-dependent effects. Therefore, we chose to evaluate them as a representative model for investigating the sensitivity and selectivity of ATA100 for ROS detection.

Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity was relatively distinctive after the uptake in chondrocyte cells when compared to that with the A549 cancer cells ( Fig. 8a). Cancer cells showed an increase in fluorescence intensity when interacting with the cell environment as shown in Fig. 8(b). The higher fluorescence in cancer cells may be due to enhanced metabolic activity and membrane permeability, allowing for more significant CQD accumulation. The reduced fluorescence in chondrocytes is likely due to fluorescence quenching from intracellular biomolecules, photobleaching, or CQD aggregation. The increased fluorescence intensity of ATA100 in A549 cells supports its potential use in cancer imaging, cell tracking, biosensing, and nanomedicine applications. Additionally, organelle-specific labeling and nanoparticle-based drug delivery are promising future directions. Moreover, to check the effect of fluorescence of the materials before and after the cell uptake, Fig.S4a show the fluorescence of only nanomaterial when placed on a glass slide, Fig.S4b shows the fluorescence in the cellular environment and Fig.S4c shows the plotted intensity graph. As seen in the Figure S4c, A549 cells showed increase of uptake and fluorescence intensity in both CA100 and ATA100 with a relatively higher fluorescence in ATA100. On the other hand, the fluorescence dropped with both the CQDs in chondrocyte cells. Together, our result shows the stable, and high tunable emission, and fluorescence capacity of ATA100 making it suitable for biological and bioimaging applications.

Further analysis was conducted to investigate the impact of exogenous hydrogen peroxide on fluorescence properties of ATA100 using A549 cells. We treated A549 with varying amounts of hydrogen peroxide (20 µM, 50 µM, 70 µM, 100 µM) and incubated for 30 minutes at 37ºC, followed by ATA100 treatment at the previously optimized concentration (600 µg/mL). Confocal imaging analysis suggested that the ATA100 fluorescence was enhanced with an increase in increasing hydrogen peroxide concentration ( Fig. 9a). The linear co-efficient value (R2=0.94) from Fig. 9(b) bars attained by plotting the mean integrated density of the fluorescence intensity images obtained with increasing concentration of hydrogen peroxide further verified efficacy of ATA100 as a ROS detection agent in cellular assays. Given that elevated ROS levels are hallmarks of cancer and oxidative stress-related diseases, this result underscores ATA100’s potential as an ROS-responsive nanobiosensor. Further advancement of ATA100 could enable real-time monitoring of oxidative stress in disease diagnosis and treatment (Fan et al.,2015; Zhou et al., 2020)

This study presents a one-pot solvothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs), derived from 2-aminoterephthalic acid and 1,2-ethylenediamine, resulting in a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, ATA100. This method is sustainable and scalable, achieving a quantum yield of 38 %, which improves upon conventional citric acid-based CQDs (CA100). Nitrogen doping enhances fluorescence efficiency and stability, making ATA100 suitable for bioimaging and theranostic applications. Characterization techniques such as FTIR, ¹H NMR, HR-TEM, and DLS indicate that ATA100 consists of ultrasmall nanoparticles (<5 nm) with superior optical properties. Optical studies reveal excellent photostability and tunable emission, making it ideal for biological assays and sensors. Cellular uptake assays in human chondrocyte and A549 lung cancer cells demonstrate enhanced fluorescence retention and preferential internalization in cancer cells. Additionally, ATA100’s concentration-dependent fluorescence response to hydrogen peroxide highlights its potential as a reactive oxygen species (ROS)-responsive biosensor.

Beyond biomedical applications, ATA100’s fluorescence modulation capability extends to immunosensors and chemical sensors (Augustine at al.,2020, Tian et al., 2020) for detecting biomolecules and environmental toxins with high sensitivity. Its scalable synthesis ensures cost-effective production, while tunable emission wavelengths broaden its usability across research domains. The integration of nitrogen doping into CQD synthesis paves the way for further advancements in nanomaterial engineering. Future research may explore additional functionalization strategies for tailored nanomedicine applications.

Overall, ATA100 emerges as a next-generation nanomaterial with immense industrial potential, combining sustainability, scalability, and high-performance fluorescence properties. This work underscores the critical role of nitrogen-doped CQDs in precision medicine, biosensing, and nanotherapeutics, advancing eco-friendly and multifunctional nanomaterials for cutting-edge applications.
